description: Late blight is a highly destructive disease of potato caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Under cool, wet, and humid conditions it can spread rapidly and cause severe foliage and tuber losses.
symptoms: Irregular pale green or water-soaked lesions appear on leaves
Lesions rapidly enlarge and become brown to black
White fuzzy pathogen growth may appear around lesion margins, especially on leaf undersides during humid conditions
Stems and petioles may develop dark brown or black lesions
Severely infected foliage may collapse rapidly
Tubers may develop irregular brown or purplish skin lesions
Internal tuber tissue may show reddish-brown granular decay

favorable_conditions.leaf_wetness: Prolonged leaf wetness strongly favors disease development
favorable_conditions.rainfall: Frequent rainfall and wet conditions greatly increase risk
favorable_conditions.additional_conditions: Cool nights, high relative humidity, fog, dew, and wet foliage can promote rapid epidemics

spread: Airborne spores can travel between plants and fields
Rain and wind-driven moisture can aid local dispersal
Infected seed tubers can introduce the pathogen
Volunteer potato plants and infected cull piles can act as inoculum sources
risk_factors: Poorly managed infected potato waste or cull piles
prevention: Destroy or properly manage infected cull potatoes
Monitor weather conditions associated with late blight risk
Scout fields frequently during cool and wet periods
Avoid irrigation practices that unnecessarily prolong foliage wetness
Use resistant or tolerant cultivars where locally suitable and available

immediate_actions: Treat suspected late blight as urgent because the disease can spread rapidly
Avoid moving infected plant material between fields
Seek confirmation from an agricultural expert when diagnosis is uncertain
cultural_control: Manage irrigation to reduce prolonged foliage wetness
biological_control: Biological products may provide supplementary preventive protection where registered, but should not be relied upon alone during severe late blight outbreaks

chemical_control.recommended: Preventive and targeted fungicide programs may be necessary when late blight risk is high. Product selection should account for local pathogen resistance and current recommendations.
chemical_control.warning: Late blight can develop fungicide resistance. Use only locally registered products and follow current resistance-management guidance, label directions, dosage, application interval, protective-equipment requirements, and pre-harvest intervals.

severity_guidance.low: A suspected localized lesion requires immediate inspection and confirmation because late blight can spread quickly.
severity_guidance.moderate: Multiple plants or lesions are present. Prompt integrated management and professional guidance are recommended.
severity_guidance.high: Rapid spread, stem infection, or widespread canopy collapse indicates a serious outbreak requiring urgent local expert guidance.

farmer_advice: Late blight is an urgent disease. During cool, humid, and rainy weather, inspect crops frequently and act quickly when suspicious water-soaked or rapidly expanding lesions appear.
disclaimer: AI-based identification should not be the sole basis for managing suspected late blight. Similar symptoms may have other causes. Seek confirmation when possible, especially during rapidly developing outbreaks.
